I spend my days patching the mahogany panels of the Emperor’s viewing deck, though I know the rot in the wood is the least of the decay eating this palace.
When the orbital shipyards fell silent, I began carving mourning shrines from the rare iron-wood of a world we can no longer reach.
I use my chisels to reinforce the barricades of the lower districts, turning luxury furniture into fortifications against the starving mobs.
As the planetary governors flee to the core, I am the only one left to repair the creaking supports of a library containing the laws of a dead civilization.
I carve the seals of defunct noble houses into coffin lids, ensuring that even in a crumbling empire, a man is buried with the prestige of his ghosts.
I am carefully sanding down the rough edges of the mahogany boardroom table to ensure the CEOs have a seamless surface for signing the final agreement.
I spent the morning reinforcing the structural supports in the executive lounge to handle the sudden influx of analysts and lawyers crowding the space.
I am installing sound-dampening cedar panels in the war room to ensure that the high-stakes negotiations remain strictly confidential.
I meticulously measured the floor plan to build a custom pedestal for the commemorative trophy that marks the union of these two corporate empires.
I am replacing the warped floorboards in the lobby so that the arriving shareholders don't trip while rushing toward the press conference.
I have spent three moons carving the great boundary totems to mark our territory, yet the rival clan dares to hack them down in an act of open war.
While the warriors sharpen their spears, I am the one who must shape the heavy timber frames for the new fortifications to keep our children safe.
I refuse to build a ceremonial throne for a chief who would rather incite a bloody feud than negotiate a peace treaty with the valley people.
My hands are calloused from crafting the sturdy rafts we used to sneak across the river for the midnight ambush.
I can tell by the precise, jagged cuts in the watchtower beams that the saboteur was trained by the same master woodworker as I was.
I carve intricate conduits into the city's living obsidian skin to ensure the sentient pulse flows without interruption.
When the urban consciousness suffers a fracture, I splice synthetic timber grafts into the skyline to stabilize its drifting geometry.
I spend my cycles sanding the iridescent edges of the memory-plazas, smoothing the jagged remnants of forgotten districts.
My chisels are tuned to the frequency of the city's humming core, allowing me to shape the programmable matter into elegant, load-bearing spires.
I meticulously join the floating mahogany terraces to the city's neural spine, blending organic warmth with the cold precision of an awake metropolis.
I have spent the morning reinforcing the witness stand with ironwood joints, for the weight of a soul's guilt often makes the timber groan and buckle.
As I carve the intricate runes into the judge's gavel, I ensure the grain follows the flow of cosmic law so that every strike resonates through the afterlife.
I watch the spectral jury with a critical eye, noting how their translucent forms pass through the mahogany benches I spent three centuries perfecting.
The prosecution's arguments are flimsy and poorly joined, lacking the structural integrity of a well-cut dovetail or a solid foundation of truth.
I stand ready with my plane and chisel to smooth the rough edges of the defendant's coffin, for even the damned deserve a vessel crafted with precision.
I can feel the pressure of the abyss screaming through the bulkhead, but I’ll keep this bulkhead sealed with a custom-carved mahogany wedge until the rivets give way.
The salt-rot has eaten through the crew's quarters, so I am stripping the mahogany panels from the officer's lounge to reinforce the sagging floorboards.
I spend my hours sanding down the jagged edges of the rusted diving bells, treating the oxidised iron as if it were a stubborn piece of kiln-dried oak.
There is a rhythmic thrum in the station's ventilation shafts that tells me exactly where the support beams have warped under the weight of the ocean.
I’ve fashioned a makeshift brace from a salvaged cafeteria table to hold the leaking observation window in place, though I know no joinery can truly fight the tide.
I look at the negative space in this conceptual installation and wonder how many structural supports it would actually take to keep those floating beams from crashing down.
These raw, unpolished slabs of cedar used as sculptures make me itch to reach for my plane and smooth out those jagged edges into a perfect finish.
While the critics praise the "deconstructionist" nature of the fragmented wall, I can see exactly where the joints failed because the artisan ignored the basic rules of load-bearing.
I find it ironic that the gallery spent thousands on a piece of "found art" that is essentially just a poorly nailed shipping crate.
The minimalist white void of this exhibition space is a masterclass in drywall finishing, though I suspect the seams are hiding a few hurried patches.
I can barely keep up with the demand for pine boards as the churchyard fills with the nameless dead.
My hands are raw and blistered from hammering together the crude, makeshift carts used to collect the bodies from the gutters.
I spent the morning sealing the gaps in my neighbor's door with heavy planks, hoping to keep the miasma from seeping into his nursery.
The smell of fresh sawdust is the only thing that masks the cloying scent of decay drifting from the plague pits.
I no longer carve ornate flourishes into the lids of the caskets, for there is no time for art when the city is dying by the hour.
I tried to plane this mahogany board, but the wood grain began to loop infinitely, carving a hole straight through the floor of the workshop.
My chisel slipped, and instead of a notch in the pine, I sliced a jagged, flickering seam into the air that reveals a void of scrolling green code.
I measured the doorframe twice, yet the distance between the studs changes every time I blink, as if the room is resizing itself in real-time.
When I drove the nail into the joist, the sound didn't echo; it stuttered in a three-second loop that froze the dust motes in mid-air.
I reached for my hammer, but my hand passed right through the handle, which had momentarily lost its collision physics and turned translucent.
